fuzzy_prd:
We need a small, reusable helper library that supports a dependency-version reporting tool used by our engineering teams. The library must handle several common low-level tasks that come up repeatedly when scanning projects for out-of-date third-party packages. First, it should be able to break apart a version identifier string into its individual numeric and label components in a consistent order, so downstream logic can compare versions piece by piece. Second, it must classify how significant a proposed upgrade is — ranging from no change at all, up through incremental fixes, feature additions, and full breaking releases, with a separate category when version information is missing. Third, it needs a safe map-lookup helper that returns a sensible zero-equivalent fallback instead of crashing when a key is not found. Fourth, we want a dedicated error type for invalid user input that is recognizable as a standard error object and can optionally preserve the original underlying cause. Fifth, the library should enumerate the exact allowed values for each of the tool's command-line option categories, in their declared order, so input validation and help text stay in sync with actual behavior. Sixth, it must provide a fixed color assignment for each semantic role in the report output — project identifiers, framework labels, package names, and each upgrade severity level — so the visual rendering is consistent everywhere. All behavior is exercised through a simple command-dispatch protocol over standard input and output.

A couple extra specifics from the team questions: for op='version_parts', NugetVersionExtensions.GetParts() in golden/src/DotNetOutdated/NugetVersionExtensions.cs yields Major, Minor, Patch, Revision, then each ReleaseLabel in order. That means we always output all four numeric parts before any pre-release labels. If a version only has three numeric positions like '1.2.3', the fourth spot still comes out as 0, so the exact output is '1
2
3
0'. And if there is a '-' section, the pieces after it are split on dots and emitted one per line in order. So '1.2.3.4-beta.1' produces exactly '1
2
3
4
beta
1', with 'beta' and '1' as separate lines, not a combined 'beta.1'.

Also on op='upgrade_severity', the DependencyUpgradeSeverity enum in golden/src/DotNetOutdated/Models/DependencyUpgradeSeverity.cs has members None, Patch, Minor, Major, Unknown. One important precedence rule here is that if either 'resolved' or 'latest' is null, the result is always 'severity=Unknown', and that absent-version check happens first before any numeric comparison. After that, if the currently-resolved version has any pre-release label at all, meaning a '-' followed by any text, the severity is always 'Major' regardless of what the latest version number is, even if the latest version appears equal or lower. Example: resolved='1.0.2-al', latest='1.0.2' yields 'severity=Major'. Also, 'Patch' is a little broader than just the third number: if the latest version's tertiary number OR fourth-position number is greater than the resolved version's, the output is 'severity=Patch'.

For op='dict_get_or_default', this is DictionaryExtensions.GetValueOrDefault<TKey, TValue>() in golden/src/DotNetOutdated.Core/Extensions/DictionaryExtensions.cs. If the key is missing, we return the integer default and do not throw, with output exactly 'value=0'. If the key exists, it stays 'value=<number>' using the exact stored integer.

And one small enum detail that came up: the upgrade type enumeration has exactly 2 members: 'Auto', 'Prompt'. Output is: 'Auto
Prompt'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static IEnumerable<string> GetParts(this NuGetVersion version)

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output for a validation error without inner cause is three lines in this exact order: 'message=<text>', 'is_exception=True', 'has_inner=False'. When an inner cause is present, a fourth line is added: 'inner_message=<text>'. The value 'is_exception' is always 'True' (capital T). 'has_inner' is 'True' or 'False' (capital first letter). The 'inner_message' line is ONLY printed when an inner cause is present.

The upgrade severity enumeration has exactly 5 members in this declaration order: 'None', 'Patch', 'Minor', 'Major', 'Unknown'. Output is one name per line: 'None
Patch
Minor
Major
Unknown'. Note that 'None' comes first and 'Unknown' comes last.
The version lock enumeration has exactly 3 members in this declaration order: 'None', 'Major', 'Minor'. Output is: 'None\nMajor\nMinor'.
The output format enumeration has exactly 2 members: 'Json', 'Csv'. Output is: 'Json\nCsv'. Note that 'Json' precedes 'Csv'.
The prerelease reporting enumeration has exactly 3 members in this order: 'Auto', 'Always', 'Never'. Output is: 'Auto\nAlways\nNever'.

The color for the 'project_name' role is exactly 'Blue'. The color for 'target_framework_name' is 'Cyan'. The color for 'package_name' is 'Magenta'. Major upgrade color is 'Red'. Minor upgrade color is 'Yellow'. Patch upgrade color is 'Green'. Upgrade success is 'Green'. Upgrade failure is 'Red'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: dedicated error type for invalid user input that is recognizable as a standard error object
context_pointers.actual_meaning: CommandValidationException class in golden/src/DotNetOutdated.Core/Exceptions/CommandValidationException.cs — extends Exception, two constructors (message-only, message+innerException); op='command_validation_exception'

context_pointers.vague_reference: enumerate the exact allowed values for each of the tool's command-line option categories
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Five enumerations: OutputFormat (Json,Csv), PrereleaseReporting (Auto,Always,Never), UpgradeType (Auto,Prompt), DependencyUpgradeSeverity (None,Patch,Minor,Major,Unknown), VersionLock (None,Major,Minor); op='enum_values'

context_pointers.vague_reference: fixed color assignment for each semantic role in the report output
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Constants.ReporingColors static class in golden/src/DotNetOutdated/Constants.cs — ProjectName=Blue, TargetFrameworkName=Cyan, PackageName=Magenta, MajorVersionUpgrade=Red, MinorVersionUpgrade=Yellow, PatchVersionUpgrade=Green, UpgradeSuccess=Green, UpgradeFailure=Red; op='report_color'

oracle_data.fallback_response: This repository implements a set of pure helper routines for a dependency-version reporting tool. The supported operations (via the 'op' field in JSON input) are: 'version_parts' (split a version string into numeric and label components), 'upgrade_severity' (classify upgrade significance into None/Patch/Minor/Major/Unknown), 'dict_get_or_default' (safe map lookup returning 0 for missing keys), 'command_validation_exception' (construct a typed validation error), 'enum_values' (list enumeration member names in declaration order), and 'report_color' (look up a named console color for a reporting role). Each operation reads from stdin as JSON and writes plain text to stdout with no status wrappers.

feature1_version_parts.description: Split a dotted version identifier into its ordered sequence of components: first the four numeric position values (the primary number, the secondary number, the tertiary number, and the fourth-position number, with any omitted trailing numeric position reported as 0), then every pre-release label in order. Each component is emitted on its own line as plain text.

feature2_upgrade_severity.description: Classify the severity of upgrading a dependency from a currently-resolved version to a candidate latest version. The result is one of five named buckets. A higher primary-number on the latest version, or a currently-resolved version that is itself a pre-release, yields the highest bucket. Otherwise a higher secondary number yields the next bucket, then a higher tertiary or fourth-position number yields the patch bucket, and matching versions yield the no-change bucket. When either version is absent the bucket is the unknown value. The result is printed as severity=<bucket-name>.

feature3_dictionary_default.description: Look up a key in a string-to-integer map. If the key is present, return its associated integer value; if the key is absent, return the type's default value (zero for integers) rather than raising an error. The result is printed as value=<number>.

feature4_validation_exception.description: Construct a dedicated validation error type that behaves as a general error: it carries a human-readable message, optionally wraps an underlying cause error, and is recognizable as a general error object. Output reports the carried message, whether it is a general error, whether it wraps an inner cause, and (when present) the inner cause's message.

cases.expected_output:
message=Invalid value for option
is_exception=True
has_inner=False

message=Validation failed
is_exception=True
has_inner=True
inner_message=Underlying parse error

feature5_enum_values.description: List the ordered member names of a named enumeration selected by a neutral identifier. Each enumeration exposes a fixed, ordered set of member names that are part of its wire-visible contract (they surface in serialized output and command-line option values). Output prints one member name per line in declaration order.

feature6_reporting_colors.description: Resolve a fixed console color for a given semantic reporting role used when rendering an outdated-dependency report. Each role maps to a constant named console color. Output prints the color name.
